title: Universal Information-Theoretic Structure of the Quasi-Stationary Domany--Kinzel Automaton
authors: Hyun-Yong Lee, Kenji Harada, Naoki Kawashima
abstract: We characterize the quasi-stationary distribution (QSD) of the bond directed-percolation line of the Domany--Kinzel automaton using a matrix-product-state representation of the probability distribution, obtained by projecting out the absorbing state and iterating the transfer matrix. Unlike moment- or sampling-based methods, this yields the full conditional distribution and direct access to information-theoretic diagnostics. The spatial structure of the QSD changes sharply across the transition: the active phase is bulk-like with finite density, whereas in the inactive phase the surviving activity collapses into a single flock occupying a vanishing fraction of the chain, with an internal filling that ranges from a single cluster deep in the inactive phase to a loose, partially filled group near criticality. This picture carries a sharp information-theoretic signature: throughout the inactive phase the bipartite mutual information of the QSD equals the entropy of a single binary choice -- whether the flock lies to the left or right of the cut -- so the surviving clusters together encode just one bit of positional information, corresponding to a single effective cluster. The approach extends matrix-product-state techniques to the projected eigenvector defining a QSD, opening information-theoretic diagnostics for absorbing-state systems that bulk-observable methods cannot reach.
summary: This work develops a sophisticated MPS framework to analyze the quasi-stationary distribution of directed percolation. By applying bipartite mutual information diagnostics, the authors prove that even when geometrically fragmented into multiple clusters, the surviving activity statistically behaves as a single, coherent 'flock' object in the subcritical regime.
main_problem: The study characterizes the quasi-stationary distribution (QSD) of the bond directed-percolation line in the Domany--Kinzel automaton and develops advanced information-theoretic diagnostics for this state.
main_result: The QSD in the inactive phase behaves as a single bound 'flock' object, which is confirmed by mutual information scaling that collapses to a universal function corresponding to one bit of positional information.
method: The authors employ Matrix-Product-State (MPS) representations and tensor network techniques to calculate the full conditional distribution, enabling diagnostics like bipartite mutual information inaccessible via moment methods.
model_or_system: The system is the Domany--Kinzel (DK) automaton on a 1D chain, modeling directed percolation. The analysis focuses on the QSD, which describes the probability distribution conditioned on non-absorption.
key_observables: Bipartite mutual information $I(\ell, N)$, cluster size distribution $P(s)$, projected dominant eigenvalue $\lambda_1$, and correlation length $\xi_{\perp}(p)$.
important_parameters: The percolation probability $p$ (relative to the critical point $p_c$), system size $N$, and cut position fraction $x = \ell/N$.
main_assumptions: Key assumptions include the 'Translated localized-droplet decomposition' for the QSD structure, which allows separating universal geometric features from amplitude dependence.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the convergence of $\lambda_1$ to an $N$-independent limit; comparisons show distinct clustering signatures ($R_{11}, P(s)$) between active and inactive phases; and diagrams detail the tensor network contractions used for calculation.
paper_structure: The paper builds from establishing the MPS framework for QSD calculation, through deriving information-theoretic diagnostics like mutual information, to finally proving that this information structure collapses onto a universal form characteristic of a single bound object in the inactive phase.
why_interesting: While focused on classical statistical mechanics models (percolation), the rigorous application of tensor networks and information theory provides powerful tools for analyzing non-equilibrium steady states in quantum systems, particularly those exhibiting absorbing or metastable dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. QSD statistics of the total active-site count 𝑛= Í 𝑖𝑥𝑖. (a) mean active count ⟨𝑛⟩versus 𝑝on a log scale, for 𝑁= 128, 256, 512, 1024. Across the inactive phase (𝑝< 𝑝𝑐) the four curves collapse onto a common, 𝑁-independent value; across the active phase (𝑝> 𝑝𝑐) the curves separate and ⟨𝑛⟩grows with 𝑁, con- sistent with a finite intensive density. A dashed vertical line marks 𝑝𝑐. (b) probability distribution 𝑃(𝑛) at 𝑝= 0.60 for the same 𝑁 values; the curves collapse onto a common limit shape, showing that the 𝑁-independence of the active count holds at the level of the full distribution, not just the mean.

FIG. 2. Clustering signatures of the QSD. (a) Nearest-neighbor clus- tering ratio 𝑅11 [Eq. (1)] versus 𝑝for 𝑁= 128, 256, 512, 1024. In the inactive phase 𝑅11 ≫1 and grows with 𝑁; in the active phase 𝑅11 →1, the value expected for independent active sites. The vertical dashed line marks 𝑝𝑐. (b) Cluster-size distribution 𝑃(𝑠) at 𝑝= 0.60 (inactive, black) and 𝑝= 0.70 (active, red), each compared with the Bernoulli baseline (1 −¯𝜌) ¯𝜌𝑠−1 of the same one-site density (dotted). The inactive distribution differs sharply from Bernoulli, signaling strong clustering, while the active distribution nearly coincides with Bernoulli, consistent with a weakly correlated bulk gas.

FIG. 3. Flock morphology in the inactive phase at 𝑝 = 0.55, 0.60, 0.62 for 𝑁= 1024. (a) Spatial-extent distribution 𝑃(ℓact): unimodal with a sharp peak at small ℓthat broadens and shifts to larger ℓas 𝑝→𝑝𝑐, with an approximately exponential tail. (b) Cumulative filling distribution 𝑃(𝜌flock < 𝑥); the strict inequality excludes single-cluster samples, so the deficit from 1 at 𝑥= 1 equals the single-cluster fraction 𝑃(𝜌flock = 1). Approaching 𝑝𝑐this deficit shrinks (0.64 →0.38 →0.19) and the curve shifts to smaller 𝜌flock, i.e. the flock loosens.

FIG. 4. Mutual-information diagnostics of the QSD, with all entropies in bits (log2). (a) Bond-resolved 𝐼(ℓ, 𝑁) at 𝑝= 0.60 and 𝑁= 1024 (inactive) versus cut position 𝑥= ℓ/𝑁, closely following ℎ(𝑥) (red) and peaking at ℎ(1/2) = 1. Inset: half-chain 𝐼half ≡𝐼(𝑁/2, 𝑁) versus 𝑝at 𝑁= 1024, with a plateau near 1 in the inactive phase that drops sharply across 𝑝𝑐(dash-dotted line) and decays in the active phase. (b) Finite-size scaling of Δ𝑘MI ≡𝑘MI −1 versus 𝑁at 𝑝= 0.55 and 0.60, consistent with 𝑘MI →1 as 𝑁→∞.
